title: ADMP Data Subject Mechanisms — Section 7 Notice + Section 38 Withdrawal
description: The April 2026 issued ADMP Guideline does NOT name 'Right to Refuse', 'Right to Information', and 'Right to Human Review' as the PCP did. The issued guideline anchors data subject mechanisms in the existing Act 709 statute: Section 7 (notice and choice) and Section 38 (withdrawal of consent). PCP-derived rights are flagged below as cross-jurisdictional best practice, NOT JPDP-issued requirements.

guidelineStatus.issuedReference: Garis Panduan ADMP, JPDP April 2026, Sections 9.2–9.10
guidelineStatus.criticalDivergence: The PCP's three named 'ADM Restrictions' framework was REPLACED by anchoring in s7 and s38. This is the most important divergence in the document.

issuedRequirements.description: What the issued guideline actually obligates. Cite these for findings.

mechanisms.name: ADM/Profiling Notification (Section 7 + §9.3)
mechanisms.rule: Mandatory ('hendaklah'). The data controller must, by written notice, notify the data subject that ADM/profiling is involved in the processing.
mechanisms.scope: The FACT of ADM/profiling is mandatorily disclosed.
mechanisms.bmQuote: Sekiranya pemprosesan tersebut turut melibatkan ADM atau Pemprofilan, pengawal data hendaklah memaklumkan perkara tersebut

mechanisms.name: ADM/Profiling Explanation (§9.4)
mechanisms.rule: Permissive ('boleh'). The data controller may, by written notice, explain the type of decisions made through ADM, the reasons for the decisions, and the likely impact. Information provided shall be reasonable and practical, but NEED NOT include any confidential information, trade secrets, intellectual property, or proprietary rights.
mechanisms.scope: Logic, reasons, and likely impact may be disclosed at the controller's discretion, subject to substantial commercial carve-outs.

mechanisms.name: Withdrawal of Consent (Section 38 + §9.6–9.8)
mechanisms.rule: Where consent is the lawful basis, data subjects may withdraw consent under s38. The withdrawal mechanism must be as easy to use as the mechanism for giving consent.
mechanisms.scope: Operates as the 'opt-out' mechanism for ADMP where consent is the basis. Where contract necessity or legal compliance is the basis, withdrawal does not apply.

sensitivePersonalData.rule: Automated decision-making involving sensitive personal data is governed by Section 40 of Act 709. Process only where (a) the data subject has given EXPLICIT CONSENT (s40(1)(a) — 'persetujuan secara nyata') OR (b) one of the s40(1)(b) conditions applies: necessary for employment law obligation, vital interests of the data subject, medical purposes, legal proceedings or legal advice. The issued guideline (§8.2) says controllers 'boleh mempertimbangkan' (may consider) implementing technical (encryption, access controls) and organisational protective measures — permissive, not mandatory.
sensitivePersonalData.explicitConsentRequirement: Section 8.2 requires 'persetujuan secara nyata' (explicit consent) for sensitive PD ADMP. NOTE: For the general ADMP Exception under §9.9.3, the issued guideline uses plain 'persetujuan' (consent) — NOT 'persetujuan secara nyata'. PCP §2.18's proposal to require explicit consent for the general ADMP Exception was NOT adopted.
sensitivePersonalData.deviationFromPCP: PCP §2.17 said 'public interest' and 'suitable measures... in place' (mandatory). Issued guideline replaces 'public interest' with the s40(1)(b) list and softens safeguards to permissive.

pcpDerivedRightsForReference.description: The PCP's three named 'ADM Restrictions' did NOT survive into the issued guideline. They are retained here for cross-jurisdictional alignment (GDPR Article 22, UK GDPR, ICO guidance). DO NOT cite the JPDP ADMP Guideline for these — cite the actual sources of the obligation (Act 709 s7, s38, s40 for issued; or external standards for analyst guidance).

rights.name: Right to Refuse (PCP-derived only)
rights.summary: PCP §2.13(a) proposed a standalone Right to Refuse to be subjected to a decision based solely on automated decision-making producing legal/significant effects. The issued guideline does NOT adopt this as a standalone right. Where consent is the basis, s38 withdrawal of consent is the operative mechanism. Where contract or legal compliance is the basis, no refusal right is created.
rights.status: NOT in issued JPDP guideline. Cross-reference GDPR Article 22(1) for the EU-style equivalent.

rights.name: Right to Information (PCP-derived doctrine partially adopted)
rights.summary: PCP §2.13(b) and §2.16 proposed mandatory disclosure of meaningful logic, even where Exceptions apply. The issued guideline §9.3 mandates only the FACT of ADM/profiling disclosure; §9.4 makes logic/reasons/impact PERMISSIVE with trade-secret/IP carve-outs. The 'retained even under Exceptions' doctrine is not stated in the issued text.
rights.status: Partially adopted. Cite §9.3 (mandatory: fact of ADM) and §9.4 (permissive: explanation), NOT the PCP doctrine.

rights.name: Right to Human Review (PCP-derived only)
rights.summary: PCP §2.13(c) proposed a Right to Human Review with an illustrative 21-day SLA + 14-day extension. The issued guideline does NOT contain a Right to Human Review or any SLA. Treat as cross-jurisdictional best practice (GDPR Article 22(3)) — DO NOT cite the JPDP ADMP Guideline.

implementationGuidance: Maintain a log of all in-scope ADMP operations with the basis for any decision (model version, input data, output, override/review history)
Update the privacy notice (s7) to disclose the FACT of ADM/profiling for every in-scope operation
Where the controller chooses to provide explanation, build it into the same system that produces the decision; document trade-secret/IP redactions
Where consent is the lawful basis, ensure the s38 withdrawal mechanism is operational and as easy as giving consent
Where sensitive PD is processed via ADMP, document the s40 lawful condition relied upon (explicit consent or s40(1)(b) ground)
Engage DPO from earliest design stage; trigger DPIA regardless of subject count
